---
title: "SO_P012: Configuration Hierarchy Consistency Principle"
subtitle: "Maintaining conceptual level consistency and domain separation in configuration files"
chapter: "CH01"
category: "principle"
number: "SO_P012"
date-created: "2025-04-13"
date-modified: "2025-08-17"
author: "Claude"
type: "principle"
law: "Structure and Organization Law"
article-number: "Article 12"
related_to:
  - "CH00.MP041: Type-Dependent Operations"
  - "CH00.MP031: Initialization First"
  - "SO_P010: Config-Driven Customization"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
---

# Article Overview {#overview}

**Structure and Organization Law Article 12 (Configuration Hierarchy Consistency Principle)**

Configuration hierarchies should maintain consistent conceptual levels within each nesting tier, with settings organized by domain rather than by component, and with sensible defaults reducing the need for explicit configuration.

:::{.callout-important}
## Core Principle
Each level of configuration structure should represent a consistent conceptual level, domain separation is preferred over component mixing, and convention over configuration.
:::

# Section 1: Theoretical Foundation {#rationale}

## Subsection 1: Cognitive Coherence {#cognitive-coherence}

Each level of the configuration hierarchy should represent a consistent conceptual level:
- Avoid mixing different concepts at the same level
- Maintain clear mental models
- Reduce cognitive burden for understanding and maintenance

## Subsection 2: Domain Separation Principle {#domain-separation}

Settings from different domains should be separated:
1. **UI Display Settings**: Labels, styles, layouts
2. **Functional Settings**: Business logic, calculation rules
3. **Data Connection Settings**: Data sources, query parameters

## Subsection 3: Convention Over Configuration {#convention-over-configuration}

- Infer sensible defaults from names and context
- Only require explicit configuration for non-standard settings
- Reduce boilerplate configuration code

# Section 2: Organization Patterns {#organization-patterns}

## Subsection 1: Horizontal Domain Separation {#horizontal-separation}

Organize by domain first, components second:

```yaml
# Horizontal separation: Domain -> Component
ui:
  component_a:
    label: "Component A"
    icon: "chart"
  component_b:
    label: "Component B"
    icon: "table"
    
data:
  component_a:
    source: "table_a"
    filters: ["active"]
  component_b:
    source: "table_b"
    filters: ["recent"]
    
functionality:
  component_a:
    calculation: "sum"
    aggregation: "daily"
  component_b:
    calculation: "average"
    aggregation: "monthly"
```

## Subsection 2: Vertical Component Separation {#vertical-separation}

Organize by component first, domain second:

```yaml
# Vertical separation: Component -> Domain
components:
  component_a:
    ui:
      label: "Component A"
      icon: "chart"
    data:
      source: "table_a"
      filters: ["active"]
    functionality:
      calculation: "sum"
      aggregation: "daily"
      
  component_b:
    ui:
      label: "Component B"
      icon: "table"
    data:
      source: "table_b"
      filters: ["recent"]
    functionality:
      calculation: "average"
      aggregation: "monthly"
```

## Subsection 3: Selection Guide {#selection-guide}

| Situation | Recommended Pattern | Reason |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Multi-component shared settings | Horizontal separation | Easier to manage common settings |
| Highly independent components | Vertical separation | Better encapsulation |
| UI-focused applications | Horizontal separation | Centralized UI settings management |
| Complex functionality | Vertical separation | Cohesive functional logic |

# Section 3: Default Application {#default-application}

## Subsection 1: Naming Convention Inference {#naming-inference}

```yaml
# Defaults based on naming
components:
  # microCustomer automatically infers:
  # - Label: "Customer"
  # - Icon: "user"
  # - Table: "customers"
  microCustomer:
    # Only configure non-standard parts
    special_filter: "vip_only"
    
  # Non-standard component needs explicit configuration
  specialAnalysis:
    ui:
      label: "Special Analysis Tool"  # Override default "Special Analysis"
      icon: "microscope"              # Cannot infer from name
    data:
      source: "complex_view"          # Non-standard data source
```

## Subsection 2: Default Hierarchy {#default-hierarchy}

```r
# Default application hierarchy
get_component_config <- function(component_name, config) {
  # 1. System defaults
  defaults <- list(
    ui = list(
      label = humanize(component_name),
      icon = infer_icon(component_name),
      visible = TRUE
    ),
    data = list(
      source = infer_table(component_name),
      cache = TRUE
    )
  )
  
  # 2. Global configuration overrides
  if (!is.null(config$global)) {
    defaults <- merge_configs(defaults, config$global)
  }
  
  # 3. Component-specific configuration overrides
  if (!is.null(config$components[[component_name]])) {
    defaults <- merge_configs(defaults, config$components[[component_name]])
  }
  
  defaults
}
```

# Section 4: Configuration Schema Validation {#schema-validation}

## Subsection 1: Schema Definition {#schema-definition}

```yaml
# Configuration schema definition
schema:
  type: object
  properties:
    ui:
      type: object
      properties:
        label:
          type: string
          description: "Display label"
        icon:
          type: string
          enum: ["chart", "table", "user", "settings"]
    data:
      type: object
      properties:
        source:
          type: string
          required: true
        filters:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
  required: ["data"]  # Data settings are required
```

## Subsection 2: Validation Implementation {#validation-implementation}

```r
# Configuration validation function
validate_config <- function(config, schema) {
  errors <- list()
  
  # Check required fields
  for (field in schema$required) {
    if (is.null(config[[field]])) {
      errors <- append(errors, 
        paste("Missing required field:", field))
    }
  }
  
  # Check type consistency
  for (field in names(config)) {
    if (!is.null(schema$properties[[field]])) {
      expected_type <- schema$properties[[field]]$type
      actual_type <- class(config[[field]])[1]
      
      if (!check_type_match(actual_type, expected_type)) {
        errors <- append(errors,
          paste("Type mismatch:", field, 
                "expected", expected_type, 
                "actual", actual_type))
      }
    }
  }
  
  if (length(errors) > 0) {
    stop(paste("Configuration validation failed:\n", 
               paste(errors, collapse = "\n")))
  }
  
  TRUE
}
```

# Section 5: Anti-Pattern Identification {#anti-patterns}

## Subsection 1: Mixed Levels {#mixed-levels}

**❌ Wrong: Mixing different concepts at same level**
```yaml
components:
  micro:
    display: "Customer Analysis"  # UI setting
    customer_profile: "db_table"  # Data setting
    color: "blue"                 # UI setting
    query: "SELECT * FROM..."     # Data setting
```

**✅ Correct: Clear concept separation**
```yaml
components:
  micro:
    ui:
      display: "Customer Analysis"
      color: "blue"
    data:
      table: "customer_profile"
      query: "SELECT * FROM..."
```

## Subsection 2: Over-Configuration {#over-configuration}

**❌ Wrong: Configuring inferable defaults**
```yaml
components:
  microCustomer:
    ui:
      label: "Customer"      # Can be inferred from name
      icon: "user"          # Can be inferred from name
      visible: true         # Default value
    data:
      source: "customers"   # Can be inferred from name
      cache: true          # Default value
```

**✅ Correct: Only configure non-standard settings**
```yaml
components:
  microCustomer:
    data:
      additional_filters: ["vip_only"]  # Non-standard setting
```

# Section 6: Best Practices {#best-practices}

## Subsection 1: Progressive Detail {#progressive-detail}

```yaml
# Start with essential settings, add detail progressively
# Level 1: Minimal configuration
components:
  micro:
    source: "customers"

# Level 2: Add common settings
components:
  micro:
    source: "customers"
    filters: ["active"]

# Level 3: Full configuration
components:
  micro:
    data:
      source: "customers"
      filters: ["active"]
      cache_duration: 3600
    ui:
      custom_label: "VIP Customers"
    functionality:
      special_calculation: "weighted_average"
```

## Subsection 2: Feature Discoverability {#feature-discoverability}

Configuration structure should clearly indicate what can be configured:

```yaml
# Clear configuration options
components:
  available_components:
    - microCustomer
    - microProduct
    - macroTrend
    
  # Explicit configurable options for each component
  microCustomer:
    configurable:
      ui: ["label", "icon", "color_scheme"]
      data: ["source", "filters", "refresh_rate"]
      functionality: ["calculations", "aggregations"]
```

# Implementation Checklist {#checklist}

:::{.callout-note}
## Configuration Hierarchy Consistency Checklist

**Structure Design**:
- [ ] Choose horizontal or vertical separation pattern
- [ ] Maintain consistency with chosen pattern
- [ ] Clear concepts at each level
- [ ] Explicit domain separation

**Default Management**:
- [ ] Name-based default definitions
- [ ] Clear default hierarchy
- [ ] Configure only non-standard settings
- [ ] Document default behaviors

**Validation Mechanism**:
- [ ] Configuration schema defined
- [ ] Validation function implemented
- [ ] Clear error messages
- [ ] Complete type checking

**Maintainability**:
- [ ] Intuitive configuration structure
- [ ] High feature discoverability
- [ ] Complete documentation
- [ ] Sufficient examples
:::

# Conclusion {#conclusion}

The Configuration Hierarchy Consistency Principle ensures configuration files are clear, understandable, and maintainable. By maintaining conceptual level consistency, appropriate domain separation, and sensible default application, you can create configuration systems that are both flexible and concise. Remember, good configuration design should make common cases simple while keeping complex cases possible.

---

*Based on: Structure and Organization Law Article 12*  
*Related to: [MP041 Type-Dependent Operations](../../CH00_fundamental_principles/03_development_methodology/MP041_type_dependent_operations.qmd), [SO_P010 Config-Driven Customization](SO_P010_config_driven_customization.qmd)*
